title: Generated artifacts and releases

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A release archive is named with version and commit, hashed, scanned, and stored by CI rather than committed into the source branch. Your goal is to define and test an artifact policy for a small project. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Ignore policy; Commits and history; Build and task commands; Tags and release points. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: Build artifacts are generated outputs with traceable source revision, toolchain, dependencies, checksums, and retention rules; they are not automatically source-of-truth files. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Some generated code or vendored assets may be intentionally versioned, while large, signed, or platform-specific artifacts need dedicated storage and provenance. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: Every generated file should be ignored, or alternatively every build output should be committed so users can find it.

includes: Distinguish reproducible outputs from source and attach verified artifacts to versioned releases rather than ordinary commits.
Define and test an artifact policy for a small project.
excludes: Some generated code or vendored assets may be intentionally versioned, while large, signed, or platform-specific artifacts need dedicated storage and provenance.

outcomes: Define and test an artifact policy for a small project.

examples.title: Generated artifacts and releases: direct case
examples.situation: A release archive is named with version and commit, hashed, scanned, and stored by CI rather than committed into the source branch.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: A release archive is named with version and commit, hashed, scanned, and stored by CI rather than committed into the source branch.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Classify each output by reproducibility and distribution need, document the policy, and preserve provenance without polluting source history unnecessarily.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Classify each output by reproducibility and distribution need, document the policy, and preserve provenance without polluting source history unnecessarily.

examples.title: Generated artifacts and releases: boundary case
examples.situation: A generated API client is committed only because project policy requires review and reproducible regeneration from a versioned schema.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: A generated API client is committed only because project policy requires review and reproducible regeneration from a versioned schema.
Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Some generated code or vendored assets may be intentionally versioned, while large, signed, or platform-specific artifacts need dedicated storage and provenance.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: Every generated file should be ignored, or alternatively every build output should be committed so users can find it.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Define and test an artifact policy for a small project.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Every generated file should be ignored, or alternatively every build output should be committed so users can find it.
misconceptions.correction: Classify each output by reproducibility and distribution need, document the policy, and preserve provenance without polluting source history unnecessarily.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: Every generated file should be ignored, or alternatively every build output should be committed so users can find it.

analogy.example: A recipe and packing line can model Generated artifacts and releases: declared ingredients resolve to exact supplies, controlled steps produce an artifact, and labels preserve provenance.
usefulFor: Understanding dependency resolution, configuration, repeatable builds, and generated artifacts
limitations: Software supply chains include executable dependencies, caches, signatures, platform variance, and secret handling beyond a kitchen process.
Some generated code or vendored assets may be intentionally versioned, while large, signed, or platform-specific artifacts need dedicated storage and provenance.

recap.summary: You should now be able to define and test an artifact policy for a small project. Keep this boundary in view: Some generated code or vendored assets may be intentionally versioned, while large, signed, or platform-specific artifacts need dedicated storage and provenance.
